record_office_terminology.land_records: Recorder of Deeds (US-period deeds, mortgages -- one office per district); Danish-period land records (matrikler) at Danish National Archives, Copenhagen
record_office_terminology.probate: USVI Superior Court (US-period probate from 1917); Danish-period probate (skifteprotokoller) at Danish National Archives
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: USVI Superior Court (US-period marriage licenses from 1917); Danish-period marriages recorded in church registers and some civil registers
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1917 (US administration began; civil vital registration under US rule. Pre-1917 vital events documented primarily in church records -- Lutheran, Moravian, Catholic, Dutch Reformed, and Jewish congregations)

burned_county_authority: The USVI has no burned districts. Record survival challenges stem from hurricane damage, colonial-era fires in Charlotte Amalie (1804, 1806, 1826), the 1878 Fireburn labor revolt on St. Croix, and the transfer of Danish colonial records to Copenhagen in 1917. The split between Danish National Archives and US repositories creates a dual-archive challenge for researchers.
administrative_notes: The US Virgin Islands (USVI) are an unincorporated US territory consisting of 3 main islands organized as districts: St. Croix, St. John, and St. Thomas. The islands were colonized by Denmark as the Danish West Indies (Dansk Vestindien) beginning in 1666 (St. Thomas), 1718 (St. John), and 1733 (St. Croix, acquired from France). Earlier periods saw Dutch, French, and English colonial activity. The Danish colonial period (1666-1917) produced records in Danish that are now held at the Danish National Archives (Rigsarkivet) in Copenhagen. Danish Governor Peter von Scholten proclaimed emancipation of enslaved persons on July 3, 1848 -- nearly two decades before the US 13th Amendment. The United States purchased the islands from Denmark on March 31, 1917, for $25 million, primarily for strategic naval purposes. US citizenship was granted to USVI residents in 1927. Genealogical research in the USVI requires working across two national archives (Danish and American), multiple church denominations (Moravian, Lutheran, Catholic, Dutch Reformed, Anglican, Jewish), and both Danish and English language records. The sugar plantation economy made the USVI a significant site in the transatlantic slave trade, and enslaved persons' records -- particularly the Danish matrikler system -- provide unusually detailed documentation compared to many slaveholding jurisdictions.

core_repositories.source_name: Danish National Archives (Rigsarkivet) -- Virgin Islands Collections
core_repositories.description: The single most important repository for USVI genealogical research before 1917. Holds the records of the Danish West Indies colonial administration including: governor's office records, court records (Vestindisk Byfoged), census returns, matrikler (land and slave registers), probate records (skifteprotokoller), customs records, military records, and church registers (kirkeboger). Many records are digitized and accessible through the Danish Archival Information System (Arkivalieronline). Records are primarily in Danish with some in Dutch, French, and English.

core_repositories.source_name: National Archives (NARA) -- US Virgin Islands Records
core_repositories.repository: National Archives and Records Administration, Washington DC / College Park MD
core_repositories.description: NARA holds US-period USVI records (1917-present) in Record Group 55 (Government of the Virgin Islands) and related record groups. Includes naval administration records (1917-1931), civilian government records, immigration records, military records, and some transferred Danish colonial records. Also holds US census records for the USVI from 1917 forward.

core_repositories.source_name: Enid M. Baa Public Library -- Virgin Islands Collection
core_repositories.repository: Enid M. Baa Public Library, Charlotte Amalie, St. Thomas
core_repositories.description: The most important local history collection in the USVI. Holds manuscripts, photographs, historic newspapers (including The Daily Herald), maps, genealogical files, and reference materials on USVI history. Named for the pioneering USVI librarian and historian. Essential for St. Thomas and territory-wide research.

core_repositories.repository: Florence Williams Public Library, Christiansted, St. Croix
core_repositories.description: St. Croix's main public library holds local history collections including plantation records, historical photographs, maps, and reference materials on St. Croix history and genealogy.

familysearch_collections.description: FamilySearch has microfilmed some USVI church records, vital records, and civil records. Collections are limited compared to US states but include some Danish-period church registers and US-period vital records. Check under both 'Virgin Islands' and 'Danish West Indies' in the FamilySearch catalog.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch Wiki -- US Virgin Islands Genealogy
familysearch_collections.description: Research guide covering USVI genealogy, record availability, and strategies for navigating the dual Danish-American archival landscape. Includes links to available microfilm and digitized collections.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Chronicling America -- US Virgin Islands Newspapers
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Some digitized USVI newspapers available through Chronicling America. Historical newspapers include the Royal Danish American Gazette (1770-1802), St. Thomas Tidende, St. Croix Avis, and The Daily Herald. Contain legal notices, shipping news, estate advertisements, and vital event announcements.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Enid M. Baa Public Library -- Newspaper Collection
newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Enid M. Baa Public Library, Charlotte Amalie, St. Thomas
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: The most complete collection of USVI newspapers held locally. Includes bound volumes and microfilm of The Daily Herald and predecessor publications. Researchers should check newspapers for legal notices, obituaries, shipping arrivals, and runaway slave advertisements (pre-1848).

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Danish West Indies Slavery Records -- Danish National Archives
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: The USVI (Danish West Indies) had a sugar plantation economy dependent on enslaved African labor. The Danish colonial administration created unusually detailed records of enslaved persons through the matrikler system -- cadastral registers that enumerated enslaved persons by plantation, name, age, and origin. Additional records include slave trade documentation, manumission records, estate inventories, emancipation records from 1848, and post-emancipation labor contracts (kontraktboger). The 1733 slave revolt on St. John and the 1878 Fireburn labor revolt on St. Croix are documented in governor's correspondence and court records.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: Moravian Church Records of Enslaved and Free Persons -- Moravian Archives
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: Moravian Archives, Bethlehem, PA / Moravian Archives, Herrnhut, Germany
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: The Moravian (Unitas Fratrum) mission in the Danish West Indies was one of the earliest Protestant missions to enslaved persons. Moravian missionaries maintained detailed records of baptisms, marriages, and burials for enslaved and free congregants from the 1730s onward. These records are among the most complete genealogical sources for enslaved persons in the Caribbean and often predate official colonial record-keeping.

immigration_and_naturalization.source_name: Charlotte Amalie Port and Immigration Records
immigration_and_naturalization.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / NARA / Enid M. Baa Public Library
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Charlotte Amalie (St. Thomas) was a major Caribbean free port attracting immigrants from across Europe, the Caribbean, the Middle East, and Africa. Danish customs and port records document arriving passengers and merchants; US-period immigration records from 1917 at NARA (Record Group 85). The diverse immigrant population included Sephardic Jews (from the 1600s), French Huguenots, Scottish and English merchants, and laborers from other Caribbean islands. Naturalization records from both Danish and US periods.

military_records.source_name: USVI Military Records -- Danish and US Period
military_records.description: Danish colonial military records include garrison records, militia musters, and military correspondence. The US Navy administered the USVI from 1917-1931; naval administration records are at NARA. US-period military service records for USVI residents from WWI, WWII, Korea, and Vietnam at NARA. The USVI National Guard records are held locally. USVI residents have served in the US military since 1917.

land_and_property.source_name: USVI Land Records -- Danish Matrikler and US Deeds
land_and_property.repository: Danish National Archives, Copenhagen / USVI Recorder of Deeds / NARA
land_and_property.description: The Danish matrikler system (cadastral registers) documented plantation boundaries, owners, and enslaved labor forces from the mid-1700s. These registers are at the Danish National Archives. US-period land records from 1917 are at the USVI Recorder of Deeds offices in Christiansted (St. Croix), Cruz Bay (St. John), and Charlotte Amalie (St. Thomas). Charlotte Amalie's urban lot records are particularly detailed.

vital_records.source_name: USVI Vital Records -- Department of Health
vital_records.description: Birth, death, and marriage certificates from the US period (1917-present) at the USVI Department of Health offices on St. Thomas and St. Croix. Pre-1917 vital events are primarily documented in church records (Lutheran, Moravian, Catholic, Dutch Reformed, Jewish) and some Danish civil registers from the 1860s at the Danish National Archives. The transition from Danish to American administration in 1917 marks the beginning of systematic US-style vital registration.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Danish National Archives / Moravian Archives, Bethlehem PA / Local congregations
church_and_cemetery.description: Church records are the primary genealogical source for USVI families before US civil registration in 1917. Key denominations: Lutheran (Danish State Church, from 1666), Dutch Reformed (from 1660), Moravian (from 1730s -- the most detailed records for enslaved persons), Catholic (from 1770s), Anglican/Episcopal (from 1848), and Sephardic Jewish (from 1796). Danish State Church registers (kirkeboger) are at the Danish National Archives; Moravian records at Moravian Archives in Bethlehem, PA, and Herrnhut, Germany; local congregations hold some original records.

church_and_cemetery.repository: Local congregations / Find A Grave / USVI government
church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery records for USVI churches and public cemeteries. Notable cemeteries include the Western Cemetery and Eastern Cemetery in Charlotte Amalie, Christiansted Cemetery, and various church burial grounds. The Sephardic Jewish cemetery in Charlotte Amalie is one of the oldest in the Western Hemisphere. Find A Grave has growing but limited USVI coverage.

research_guides_and_societies.repository: VISHA / St. Croix Landmarks Society / St. John Historical Society
research_guides_and_societies.description: VISHA and local historical societies maintain research resources, publish historical studies, and preserve documentary heritage. The St. Croix Landmarks Society focuses on plantation archaeology and architectural preservation. The St. John Historical Society collects oral histories and local records.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Virgin Islands Studies Collective / University of the Virgin Islands
research_guides_and_societies.repository: University of the Virgin Islands, St. Thomas / St. Croix
research_guides_and_societies.description: The University of the Virgin Islands maintains archival collections, oral history recordings, and academic resources for USVI history research. The Caribbean Writers Project and other UVI initiatives document USVI cultural heritage.

historical_counties_extinct.description: The US Virgin Islands have no extinct districts. The three districts -- St. Croix, St. John, and St. Thomas -- correspond to the three main islands and have existed as administrative units since the Danish colonial period (1666-1917). Under Danish rule, each island had its own administrative apparatus including courts, customs offices, and church parishes. The district boundaries have remained unchanged through the transfer from Danish to American sovereignty in 1917. Minor outlying islands (Water Island, Hassel Island, etc.) are administratively part of the St. Thomas district.

burned_counties_summary.notes: The USVI has no burned districts in the traditional sense. Record survival challenges are due to: (1) hurricane damage across all three islands over centuries; (2) fires in Charlotte Amalie (1804, 1806, 1826) that destroyed portions of the town; (3) the 1878 Fireburn labor revolt on St. Croix; and (4) the transfer of Danish colonial records to Copenhagen in 1917, which physically separated records from the territory. The dual-archive situation (Danish National Archives and NARA) is the primary challenge for USVI researchers.
